sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=16).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 16, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The green+W is now at cols 15-17, overlapping the left frame's top-right corner (cols 11-15). The left frame is also damaged like the right one was. Both frames get damaged when green squares push into them. Let me try clicking the green+W when positioned at the frame to see if a special interaction occurs.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 15 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me track what happened:
Before click: green+W at cols 15-17 (center W at col 16), green+c at cols 42-44 (center c at col 43)
After click: green+W at cols 15-17 (center W at col 16), green+c at cols 42-44 (center c at col 43)
Wait, the centers didn't change! id=8 is W at [31, 16] and id=9 is c at [31, 43]. These are the same as before the click.
But wait, the board_changed was True. Let me check what changed. Looking at the small nodes:
- id=6: N, 8 pixels, boundary=[[30, 15], [30, 17], [32, 17], [32, 15]] - green square at left (same)
- id=7: N, 8 pixels, boundary=[[30, 42], [30, 44], [32, 44], [32, 42]] - green square at right (same)
- id=8: W, 1 pixel, boundary=[[31, 16]] - center of left green (same)
- id=9: c, 1 pixel, boundary=[[31, 43]] - center of right green (same)
The centers didn't change. But the board did change. Let me check the frame or other elements.
Looking at id=13: W, 10 pixels, boundary=[[63, 54], [63, 63]] - this is the bottom bar. Before it was 9 pixels at [[63, 55], [63, 63]]. Now it's 10 pixels at [[63, 54], [63, 63]]. The bottom bar extended by 1 pixel to the left!
So clicking on the green+W when it's at the frame position caused the bottom bar to extend. This might be a progress indicator. Each click at a frame position adds to the progress bar.
Let me think about this:
- The bottom bar is at row 63, extending from some column to col 63
- Initially it was shorter, now it's longer
- Clicking at the frame position extends it
Maybe the goal is to:
1. Position each green square at each frame
2. Click to "complete" the frame
3. The bottom bar tracks progress
4. When the bar is full, the level completes
But I clicked on the green+W at the left frame and the bar extended. Let me try clicking on the green+c at the right frame to see if the bar extends further.
Actually wait, let me re-examine. The green+W is at cols 15-17, and the left frame is at cols 11-15. The green+W is overlapping the frame. When I clicked, the bar extended. But the green+W's center didn't change color.
Previously, when I clicked on the green+B (not at a frame), the colors swapped. Now when I clicked on the green+W (at the frame), the colors didn't swap but the bar extended. So clicking behavior depends on whether the green square is at a frame position!
Let me try clicking on the green+c at the right frame to see if the bar extends further.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Clicking the green+W while at the left frame position caused the bottom bar to extend (from 9 to 10 pixels). The center colors did NOT swap this time - clicking at frame positions has a different effect than clicking elsewhere. This is likely a progress mechanism. Need to click the other green square at the right frame too.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the green+c at the right frame
# Green+c center is at row 31, col 43
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 43}])
print("Result:", result)

# Check the bottom bar
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nBottom bar node:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and 8 <= n['pixels'] <= 15:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check centers
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"  Center: id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
